text: Teams that rely on MQLs and things like content downloads to nurture leads are in big trouble. What works today is all going to be this zero-click marketing stuff where you have to do good marketing, do stunts, and hope that people remember you, find out about you, and come back and end up buying from you. But most marketing teams obsess over trying to track and measure and quantify everything because we need to get credit inside of the company and the priorities don't match. Buyers and potential customers want information as quickly and as easily as possible, but But the company wants to track everything and hoard everything and gatekeep everything so we can measure it and give it credit. But the customer is always going to win. So you need to match your marketing to customer behavior if you want it to survive, not match it to what you can show the CEO and CFO to prove that marketing is working. Does that message land with you at all? That is so spot on. And I was smiling as you read it because I was in that camp. I was in the camp where measure everything. Like I remember when I was leading growth at Samsara, it was like, if you want to talk to me about something that was immeasurable, it's like I'm not even going to take that meeting. I was very extreme. And I'm polar opposite now. Like maybe almost like too much. It's just like, okay, we're not going to be able to measure this thing that like we want to do, but I can see how it will capture attention. I can see how it's going to ride a wave. Like, yeah, let's do it. Like I don't care if we can't measure it. I know we will – the results, the halo effect will impact the business positively in other areas even if it's not directly attributable. Right. And I think people hear this, though. It's not that you're – we're not saying don't measure it. It's almost like when you do these things, the measurement actually becomes obvious. Measurement becomes hard when it's like there's too many micro things and we don't know if this thing worked. It's like forever I've been involved in podcasting as part of a business, and people are always like, how are you going to quantify the podcast? And I'm like, dude, if you just do it, people will tell you. You know, like I hear Ramp – I hear – it's an example of Ramp podcast ads. Like how are you quantifying the Ramp ads on like Founders Podcast, right? They tell you. They literally tell you. I heard about you. And so how does that impact like the marketing playbook at Ramp? Like I don't see a ton of like direct response stuff. I don't see a ton of like get this e-book and sales is going to call you. What are you guys going to do over there? We do all of that. The fact you don't remember it makes the point on attention and how those tactics are already commoditized Sure You remember some of the bigger splashy stuff Yeah Yeah And I mean like what I probably say is we do all of the above and we do measure it but the measurement is not the really strict nitpicky like attribution model. How many leads did this gather? Like it's more thoughtful in terms of like the experimental design of when you're doing something. Like maybe you're not measuring leads. Maybe you're measuring something else. And in general, like the larger the expected magnitude, like the less rigorous you actually need to be in measurements. Like if we're going to do a giant stunt for millions of dollars in the middle of New York, like I don't really care about the attribution model. I can just like go eyeball a chart and see how we had a giant spike that week. Like it worked and we can kind of like understand the magnitude versus to maybe your point. If I'm like changing the color on a landing page that gets a tiny amount of traffic, it's like, OK, I'm going to have to really have great experimental design to tease out if there's any signal there or not. I'd much rather do the former. It's more fun. It has a bigger impact on the business. It's where I think the market is going as well. Okay, I'm supposed to ask you about the topics in my doc. So the first one we have is like, this is AI and the death of functional marketing roles. George's view is that 80% of what marketers do today is task execution and AI is taking a lot of that. So what does that mean for the future of a marketing org? Yeah, it probably means, I mean, it probably means the job is more fun for two reasons. Number one, I think marketing becomes a lot more important as like execution and intelligence becomes commoditized and you're forced to figure out how you capture people's attention. And number two, it's also becomes more fun because you're no longer in these platforms clicking buttons or spending time reading Salesforce reports. Like you have the, like all of that is going to be done by machines and you have the luxury to sit in a room and think or work with some coworkers and like brainstorm some creative ideas. I'm good at like making the ads but I'm not good at the distribution of the ads like the segments and this and that and I've always been like let me just make the ad and then like give it to the computer and they can run all the variations of it and it's like that's exciting I have in here it's like this idea is like moving away from functional owners for example SEO lead, paid lead and email lead towards generalists who manage fleets of agents Ramp is moving to a hub and spoke model centralized agent teams built memory systems and general purpose skills. And then someone kind of leads those and runs them. Yes, exactly. And it like a hub and spoke to another hub and spoke So like we have kind of a centralized group of folks that are building tools across ramp to make us more efficient with AI But then like we have like a centralized team even within growth of like agent operators whatever you want to call them. That's what we call them, that go and take those systems and they generalize them or make them more specific, I should say, to folks on growth. And that is like memory systems. Like what is the shared memory system for growth marketing that understands all the past experiments that we've run or the context on what is a good email to us? And all of that information is kind of common across teams. But then on each sub-team, whether it's performance marketing or events or whatever, we're developing these embedded agent leads who are going to go and take these tools and make them incredibly specific to the team. So on performance marketing, it might be someone that takes these tools and they automate all of paid search campaigns. And that's been working really, really well for us so far. Can you explain some of the, if you can, is that you just built this in Cloud or is it custom? People want to know, how did you build this inside? So the general concept is basically taking all of the pieces of marketing, building an agent for that role, but what's that built on? Yeah, it is a lot of cloud code. I think that's been a very successful tool for most companies over the past six months. Some of it is tools with interfaces. I actually kind of push the team away from that. A lot of it is headless automated workflows. Uh, so a great example is like, we built a system to launch like new verticals. Um, and what does the launch new vertical mean? It's not like a super rigorous, oh, let's build a sales team or anything like that, but it can go and like get a signal in the market. It goes and does a bunch of research. It pulls customer quotes and happy customers from that vertical. It puts together a glossary of terms that might be specific to that industry or vertical. And like this giant context layer gets created. And then from that context layer, you can kick off workflows to let's set up a landing page. Let's launch ads. Let's do a direct mailer. And all that is like completely autonomous now. And that's new. That didn't exist two months ago. And so you can kind of imagine how with a system like that, it's like, OK, what's the job of the human? It's not going and clicking buttons in Google ads or going into your CMS and building a page. It's like, all right, what do we actually want to say? What's our unique position for this vertical? What is the is the TAM opportunity worth even going after it and spending the tokens on building this? And I think that's honestly like more interesting work. Yeah, that's a new language inside of the company. Like, is this worth this investment? What been the team reaction to this Like I seen a bunch of the stuff that your CEO and founders have put out about I think we talk about this in a minute like this project class and building an AI pilled company. What's the team response been to like, hey, we're changing how marketing works around here. And we're going to be this like agent first type of marketing org. Yeah, it's kind of I think everyone always knew that this was coming and felt it. like it may have become more extreme over the past like six to nine months. But again, like being part of like technically the engineering org rolling up to the CTO meant everyone that we've hired has kind of been on board in one shape or form since day one. Now, having said that, I think everyone feels behind. Like I remember going on a vacation back in November and coming back and like, oh, my God, I'm so behind. Like I'm going to get fired. Like I don't even know what some of the stuff is because things are just moving so quickly. I think that's like a normal feeling. So outside of the anxiety of feeling behind. It's nice to hear you say that. Everyone can promise you that. All the people that listen to this are collectively being like, all right, if the VP of growth at Ramp feels stressed out, and you're plugged into this, you report to the CTO, the CTO of one of the fastest growing companies in tech right now, like, okay, I could end the pod right now. That feels great. I mean, everyone feels that, I think, at every level. I think it is because things really are moving that quickly. But outside of that, the response from the team has been phenomenal. Everyone has stepped up. Folks in roles that like traditionally I would never expect to get technical are like in terminal on a flight over being like, oh, I was so irritated. What's an example of that? Of like a team like in terminal? Yeah, obviously you're referencing someone in your head, like someone that you didn't thought would be into the technical stuff. What's the example? I mean, like anyone on the events team I'm incredibly proud of. Like their role is to do in-person things. And like I shared my philosophy maybe a year, year and a half ago that like in-person interactions, high trust interactions are one of the few things that are immune to like the changes from AI. That's never going to go away. And so we should invest a lot more there. And so we scaled the events team quite a bit. The expectation was kind of set, hey, like your job is in-person stuff and we're making a large bet there. So the fact that these folks that like where their skill set really is in-person interactions and experiences are like in terminal on a plane building stuff. And it's not just one person. It's like the entire team. I've been very impressed with. I have one person on the team that has no coding experience, no technical background, and she was talking to me about the latency of this API call.
